title: Pepparkakor
subtitle: Swedish spiced ginger thins
excerpt: The traditional Swedish Christmas ginger biscuit: paper-thin, crisp, and warmly spiced with ginger, cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves. They snap cleanly and last for weeks in a tin.
sourceNotes: Pepparkakor (literally 'pepper biscuits') are the defining Swedish Christmas biscuit, baked throughout Scandinavia in the weeks before Christmas and served with coffee. The spice blend (ginger, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves) and the thin, crisp texture are fixed elements; the shapes (hearts, stars, pigs, and gingerbread people) vary by tradition. The recipe is documented in Leila Lindholm's A Piece of Cake (2008) and in the Swedish culinary tradition recorded in Trine Hahnemann's Scandinavian Baking (2014).

recipe.yieldDescription: about 60 biscuits, depending on cutter size
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked pepparkakor in a sealed bag for up to 3 months. They will soften slightly on thawing but regain crispness after 30 minutes at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Scale freely. The dough keeps in the fridge for 5 days; bake in batches as needed.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough must be chilled for at least 2 hours before rolling; it can be made up to 5 days ahead. Baked biscuits keep in a tin for 3 to 4 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; bake one tray at a time as they colour fast

content.text: The dough needs to be rolled as thin as possible: 2 mm is the target. A thick pepparkakor is not a pepparkakor but a ginger biscuit. Cold dough rolls more easily; if it warms and becomes sticky, return it to the fridge for 10 minutes. A lightly floured, cool surface and a cold rolling pin make the difference.

Bake one tray at a time and watch from the 5-minute mark. At 2 mm thick, the window between done and burnt is about 90 seconds. The biscuits should be just beginning to darken at the edges when they come out; they will crisp as they cool.
Make the dough and chill, 2 hours

Sift the {{plain-flour}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{cardamom-ground}}, {{cloves-ground}}, and {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} into a large bowl. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{caster-sugar}}. Warm the {{golden-syrup}} briefly until liquid and stir it in with the {{double-cream}}. Bring the dough together with your hands; it should be smooth and not sticky. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for at least 2 hours.
Roll and bake, 7–9 minutes per tray

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line several baking trays with baking paper. Working with one quarter of the dough at a time (keep the rest refrigerated), roll on a lightly floured surface to about 2 mm thick. Cut into shapes with cookie cutters and place on the lined trays. Bake for 7 to 9 minutes until the edges just begin to darken. The centres will still look slightly underdone. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes to firm, then transfer to a rack to cool completely. They will crisp as they cool.
